input.content:
# Plan — Helpful visuals for GOOG and ASML playbooks (patterns P18–P24) ## Context The two playbooks render asymmetrically today: - `playbooks/GOOG.html` falls through to prose fallback for all seven body sections. Only the global patterns P9 (frontmatter → meta-grid), P10 (status pills), and P4 (numeric tables) are active. - `playbooks/ASML.html` uses the same global patterns, but additionally carries **hand-authored inline CSS** for §4 (`.axis-card` / `.bull-block` / `.bear-block` / `.nvs`, lines 115–128) and §5 (`.fm` / `.fm-tag`, lines 131–132). These don't live in the pattern catalogue, so they don't transfer to other tickers and they don't follow the skeleton's dark-mode token scheme. Both observations are symptoms of the same problem: the playbook body has rich, structured content (segment mixes, margin anchors, catalyst probabilities, bull/bear/noise triplets, layer-tagged failure modes, scenario_axes asymmetry, drawdown/recovery episodes), but no patterns in the catalogue target that content. So either a reader gets prose or a future agent has to reinvent custom HTML, and the two playbooks drift apart visually each time someone re-renders them. The goal is to add seven new patterns (P18–P24) to `references/html-patterns.md`, one per body section, so that: - Every playbook body section gets a visual treatment without per-ticker custom HTML. - ASML's existing `.axis-card` / `.fm-tag` CSS is promoted into the catalogue (re-keyed to shell tokens, so dark mode works) and GOOG inherits both treatments automatically on the next render. - All data flows from playbook markdown + frontmatter only. No external lookups, no MCP calls, no signal-output reads. SVGs are inline. - New patterns activate on the current GOOG.md / ASML.md content with **zero markdown changes** — they read the shape that's already there. - Future tickers (NFLX, AMZN, META rewrite) get the same visual treatment automatically as long as their markdown follows the playbook conventions. ## Files modified **Primary edit:** - `/Users/anton/src/pde-skills/references/html-patterns.md` — append seven new pattern specs (P18 through P24) following the existing six-part structure (Name, Detect, HTML, CSS, Accessibility, When to use / when not to use). **Re-rendered (verification, not edits):** - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/GOOG.html` — re-render via `/pde-render-html playbooks/GOOG.md` after each slice lands; expect the symmetry gap with ASML to close visibly. - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.html` — re-render via `/pde-render-html playbooks/ASML.md`; expect the hand-authored inline CSS to be replaced by pattern-driven markup. The visual surface should stay near-identical to the existing ASML.html, just driven from markdown. **Read-only references:** - `/Users/anton/src/pde-skills/references/html-skeleton.md` — token reference. Patterns must use only the documented tokens (`--accent`, `--ok`, `--danger`, `--warn`, `--info`, `--muted`, `--rule`, `--surface`, `--fg`, `--bg`, and their `-soft` companions). The skeleton already handles dark mode and print. - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.html` lines 115–137 — source of the existing `.axis-card` / `.fm-tag` CSS being promoted into P21/P22. Note: existing CSS uses `var(--border)`, must be re-keyed to `var(--rule)` (the skeleton's authoritative name). - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/GOOG.md` and `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.md` — the canonical content the patterns must match. ## The seven new patterns One pattern per playbook body section. Detection rules below are summaries; full HTML/CSS specs live with the implementation. All shell tokens, all dark-mode-safe by construction. ### P18 — Segment-mix horizontal stack bar (§1) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/business architecture|segment mix|revenue mix/i` AND a table whose first column header is `Segment` and second column carries `%` / `share` / `mix`. - **Render:** Inline SVG 100%-stacked horizontal bar above the source table. Slice colours cycle through six tokens (`--accent`, `--info`, `--warn`, `--ok`, `--danger`, `--muted`). Slice labels render only on slices ≥8%; the source table below carries the full legend. - **Why bar, not donut:** Donuts hide the long tail and don't print well. The bar reads left-to-right, prints in B&W, and the table remains the canonical data source. - **Markdown change:** None. Both GOOG.md §1 and ASML.md §1 already match. ### P19 — Margin position-band strip (§2) - **Detect (strict, three concurrent conditions):** H2 matching `/(capex|AI investment) cycle|cycle position|normalised margin/i` AND a paragraph containing all of (a) a keyword like `floor / ceiling / base / anchor / sits between`, (b) at least two `%` anchors, (c) within 250 characters of each other. - **Render:** Horizontal range strip with gradient track (`--danger-soft → --warn-soft → --ok-soft`), endpoint labels for floor and ceiling, marker tick at the base anchor. Tick position computed from `(base − low) / (high − low)`. - **Why strict trigger:** DCF prose is dense with `%` figures that aren't position anchors. Falling through to prose is the correct default; only render the strip when the anchor framing is unambiguous. - **Markdown change:** None. GOOG.md §2 ("Normalised-margin anchors") and ASML.md §2 ("Normalised-margin thesis") both match. ### P20 — Catalyst probability × IV-impact mini-bars (§3) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/catalysts?/i` AND a table with headers matching `Catalyst` + `Probability` + `IV impact`. - **Render:** The existing markdown table augmented with two extra cells per row — a blue probability bar and a green/red IV-impact bar (width = midpoint of any range like `−10–20%`, capped at 30% for visual normalisation). Direction column becomes a pill (`--ok-soft / --ok` for positive, `--danger-soft / --danger` for negative, `--warn-soft / --warn` for mixed). - **Why inline mini-bars, not scatter:** 8 catalyst points on a scatter lose label legibility; mini-bars preserve identity and table-scan order. - **Markdown change:** None. Both §3 tables match. ### P21 — Bull / Bear / Noise-vs-Structural axis grid (§4) — **promotion of existing ASML CSS** - **Detect:** H2 matching `/sell[- ]side disagreement|disagreement axes/i` AND per-axis H3s where each has a `**The question:**` line followed by `**Bull:**` / `**Bear:**` / `**Noise vs. structural:**` blocks. - **Render:** Per-axis card: full-width question banner, two-column Bull/Bear pair (green-soft / red-soft tints), full-width NVS panel beneath (neutral tint). Mobile collapses to single column. - **Migration from ASML.html:** Re-key the existing `.axis-card / .bull-block / .bear-block / .nvs` classes to `.p-axis-card / .p-axis-bull / .p-axis-bear / .p-axis-nvs`; swap hardcoded hexes for `--ok-soft / --ok` (bull) and `--danger-soft / --danger` (bear). The hand-authored `
` blocks in ASML.html lines 351–467 disappear — replaced by pattern-driven markup. - **Markdown change:** None. Both playbooks already use the canonical shape. ### P22 — Layer-tagged failure-mode cards (§5) — **promotion of existing ASML CSS** - **Detect:** H2 matching `/failure modes?/i` AND a bullet list where ≥50% of items end with `[xxx layer]` tags. - **Render:** Per-mode card with a left-positioned coloured chip carrying the layer label. Auto-generated legend strip above (`aria-hidden`, since each card carries its own tag for screen readers). Fixed palette mapping nine known layers (`interface`, `monetisation`, `cost`, `ecosystem`, `demand`, `regulatory`, `technology`, `accounting`, `FX`) to specific token pairs; unknown layers fall to a muted `-other` style. - **Migration from ASML.html:** Re-key existing `.fm / .fm-tag` classes to `.p-fm-card / .p-fm-tag`; bind tag-specific colours to the layer palette. ASML.html's `
…
` block (lines 472–497) is deleted. - **Markdown change:** None. All ASML and GOOG failure-mode bullets already end with `[xxx layer]`. ### P23 — Scenario-axes shape plot (§6) — **reads from frontmatter, not body** - **Detect:** P9 frontmatter parser produces a `scenario_axes` field with bear/bull deltas on `growth_ceiling` and/or `terminal_margin`. The plot lifts into the body immediately under the §6 H2 (matched by `/overrides|rationale/i`). - **Render:** Small inline SVG plot — x-axis = growth ceiling (0–25%), y-axis = terminal margin (15–40%), three points (bear / base / bull) connected by a polygon (`fill: --accent-soft`). Reads the shape's asymmetry at a glance: structural-break shape (asymmetric polygon) vs symmetric ±delta shape (isoceles triangle). - **Fallback formulas:** When bear/bull overrides are absent, the renderer uses the same `±multiplier` formulas the DCF loader uses (documented in the catalogue entry verbatim so they don't drift). - **Markdown change:** None. Both playbooks' frontmatter already populates `scenario_axes`. ### P24 — Reset-and-recover scatter (§7) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/historical reset|reset[- ]and[- ]recover|recovery prior/i` AND a table with `Episode | Drawdown | … | Recovery | …` headers. - **Render:** Inline SVG scatter on (recovery duration in months × drawdown %). One dot per episode, coloured by severity band (`≤20% → --warn`, `20–40% → --danger`, `>40% → --danger` saturated). Source table sits below as canonical legend. - **Why scatter, not horizontal time-bar:** Source data is magnitude × duration, not calendar bounds. Scatter encodes "how deep × how long" directly. - **Markdown change:** None. Both §7 tables match. ## Slicing — three implementation slices, lowest-risk first The slicing order exercises infrastructure under known-correct conditions before any new visual logic ships, so a regression in one slice doesn't compound into the next. **Slice 1 — promotions only (P21, P22).** Lift ASML's inline CSS into the catalogue and re-key to shell tokens. No new visual logic; the rendering tree is what ASML.html already produces, just driven from markdown. Dark mode starts working everywhere it didn't before, and GOOG.html immediately gains both sections. Verification surface: 2 playbooks × 2 sections = 4 cases, each comparable to the existing ASML.html snapshot. **Slice 2 — table-driven inline charts (P18, P20, P24).** Three patterns that decorate existing markdown tables with inline SVG. Common implementation primitive: a numeric-cell parser (`~35%`, `−10–20%`, `~14 months` → numeric value). Build the parser once, then each pattern is ~30 lines of SVG generation. No detection ambiguity — each pattern has a unique header-column signature. **Slice 3 — narrative + frontmatter patterns (P19, P23).** P19 has the strictest detection rule in the set (three concurrent conditions) and consumes free-form prose; P23 reads from frontmatter and depends on P9's parser. Both are the highest-risk patterns; saving them for last gets infrastructure confidence first. Each slice is one PR / one Linear issue. A new Linear issue should be created per slice under the Equity Skill Pack project, scoped to the patterns it lands plus the re-render of GOOG.html and ASML.html for verification. ## What this plan does NOT change - **Frontmatter schema and override values.** `base_wacc`, `growth_ceiling`, `terminal_margin`, `confidence_anchor`, `scenario_axes` numerics, and narrative strings are all untouched. P23 reads them; it does not write them. - **Playbook markdown bodies.** Zero changes to GOOG.md or ASML.md. All seven detection rules trigger on the current content. - **The `/pde-render-html` skill workflow.** Steps 1–7 stay the same. P18–P24 are read as part of Step 4's pattern-table lookup, just like P1–P17 already are. - **Existing patterns P1–P17.** No edits to existing pattern entries. P18–P24 sit at the end of the catalogue and inherit the existing first-match-wins ordering. Conflicts checked: P4 (NFR table) needs `Target / SLO / p95 / Latency` headers — none appear in playbook tables. P17 (risks table) needs a `` with `Risk / Severity / Mitigation` columns — playbook failure modes are bullets, never tables. No collisions. - **Other consumers of the pattern catalogue.** Pattern names are prefixed `p-` per the catalogue convention; no class-name collisions with the host shell, the existing patterns, or hand-authored content elsewhere. - **External data sources.** No new yfinance / EDGAR / web fetches. All visual data lives in the markdown or the frontmatter the markdown already carries. ## Verification After each slice, the following must pass: 1. **Pattern catalogue parses and is consistent.** Each new entry has the six-part structure (Name, Detect, HTML, CSS, Accessibility, When to use / not). All CSS uses only documented skeleton tokens. No hex literals in pattern CSS. Verify by grepping the appended block for `#[0-9a-f]{3,6}` and confirming zero matches. 2. **Self-containment preserved.** Re-rendered GOOG.html and ASML.html have zero ``, zero `

# Plan — Reconcile ASML & GOOG playbooks to a shared quality bar

## Context

`playbooks/ASML.md` and `playbooks/GOOG.md` are the two most recently authored ticker playbooks (May 2026). They share the same 7-section structure and frontmatter schema, but the depth and analytical rigour drift unevenly:

These don't live in the pattern catalogue, so they don't transfer to other tickers and they don't follow the skeleton's dark-mode token scheme. Both observations are symptoms of the same problem: the playbook body has rich, structured content (segment mixes, margin anchors, catalyst probabilities, bull/bear/noise triplets, layer-tagged failure modes, scenario_axes asymmetry, drawdown/recovery episodes), but no patterns in the catalogue target that content. So either a reader gets prose or a future agent has to reinvent custom HTML, and the two playbooks drift apart visually each time someone re-renders them. ` block (lines 472–497) is deleted. - **Markdown change:** None. Both are the highest-risk patterns; saving them for last gets infrastructure confidence first. Each slice is one PR / one Linear issue. lines: -# Plan — Reconcile ASML & GOOG playbooks to a shared quality bar
+# Plan — Helpful visuals for GOOG and ASML playbooks (patterns P18–P24)

-`playbooks/ASML.md` and `playbooks/GOOG.md` are the two most recently authored ticker playbooks (May 2026). They share the same 7-section structure and frontmatter schema, but the depth and analytical rigour drift unevenly:

-- **GOOG §4 (Sell-Side Disagreement Axes)** uses a structured Bull / Bear / Noise-vs-Structural sub-format per axis; **ASML §4** uses one-paragraph-per-axis. This is the single largest quality gap — GOOG's "noise-vs-structural" discipline tells the reader what to ignore vs. act on, and is absent from ASML.
-- **GOOG failure_modes** carry explicit layer tags (`[interface layer]`, etc.) mapping to its §4 taxonomy; **ASML** has none.

-- **ASML §2** anchors the terminal-margin choice with three numerical brackets (TTM-clean 24% / base 30% / earnings-implied 35%); **GOOG §2 and §6** describe the same trade-off without numerical anchors.
-- **ASML bull_narrative** enumerates six discrete upside vectors (i)–(vi); **GOOG bull_narrative** is one prose paragraph.
+- `playbooks/GOOG.html` falls through to prose fallback for all seven body sections. Only the global patterns P9 (frontmatter → meta-grid), P10 (status pills), and P4 (numeric tables) are active.

+- `playbooks/ASML.html` uses the same global patterns, but additionally carries **hand-authored inline CSS** for §4 (`.axis-card` / `.bull-block` / `.bear-block` / `.nvs`, lines 115–128) and §5 (`.fm` / `.fm-tag`, lines 131–132). These don't live in the pattern catalogue, so they don't transfer to other tickers and they don't follow the skeleton's dark-mode token scheme.

-The goal is parity: a reader switching between the two playbooks should encounter the same level of structural rigour and the same depth of analysis. The intended outcome is that both files become substitutable as quality exemplars for future playbooks (NFLX, AMZN, PORTFOLIO targets).

+Both observations are symptoms of the same problem: the playbook body has rich, structured content (segment mixes, margin anchors, catalyst probabilities, bull/bear/noise triplets, layer-tagged failure modes, scenario_axes asymmetry, drawdown/recovery episodes), but no patterns in the catalogue target that content. So either a reader gets prose or a future agent has to reinvent custom HTML, and the two playbooks drift apart visually each time someone re-renders them.
+The goal is to add seven new patterns (P18–P24) to `references/html-patterns.md`, one per body section, so that:
-- The 7 body sections are **human-only narrative** — no code parses them. Body rewrites cannot break the `/stock-model` skill.

-- The YAML frontmatter is **load-bearing**. `base_wacc`, `growth_ceiling`, `terminal_margin`, `confidence_anchor`, `bear_narrative`, `base_narrative`, `bull_narrative`, `failure_modes`, `scenario_axes`, `last_updated` are all read by the loader. Frontmatter edits in this plan are limited to *string content* (narratives, failure-mode descriptions) — no schema changes, no field additions, no override-value changes.

-- HTML rendering is **manual** via the global `/pde-render-html` skill. The user explicitly framed HTML as a follow-up step ("starting with the markdown files… then later on to the friendlier HTML rendered versions"). HTML re-render is out of scope for this plan.
-- META.md is out of scope — user named only ASML and GOOG.
+- Every playbook body section gets a visual treatment without per-ticker custom HTML.
+- ASML's existing `.axis-card` / `.fm-tag` CSS is promoted into the catalogue (re-keyed to shell tokens, so dark mode works) and GOOG inherits both treatments automatically on the next render.
+- All data flows from playbook markdown + frontmatter only. No external lookups, no MCP calls, no signal-output reads. SVGs are inline.
+- New patterns activate on the current GOOG.md / ASML.md content with **zero markdown changes** — they read the shape that's already there.
+- Future tickers (NFLX, AMZN, META rewrite) get the same visual treatment automatically as long as their markdown follows the playbook conventions.

+- `/Users/anton/src/pde-skills/references/html-patterns.md` — append seven new pattern specs (P18 through P24) following the existing six-part structure (Name, Detect, HTML, CSS, Accessibility, When to use / when not to use).
-- `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/skills/stock-model/SKILL.md` — confirms which frontmatter keys are load-bearing
-- `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/META.md` — third existing playbook (for sense-checking but not modified)
+- `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/GOOG.html` — re-render via `/pde-render-html playbooks/GOOG.md` after each slice lands; expect the symmetry gap with ASML to close visibly.

+- `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.html` — re-render via `/pde-render-html playbooks/ASML.md`; expect the hand-authored inline CSS to be replaced by pattern-driven markup. The visual surface should stay near-identical to the existing ASML.html, just driven from markdown.
-### A1. §4 Sell-Side Disagreement Axes — restructure to Bull / Bear / Noise-vs-Structural per axis

-Each of the 5 existing axes (Capex-cycle trough timing; High-NA economics at scale; China revenue durability; TAM ceiling; IBM/services as moat anchor) is rewritten with three sub-sections matching the GOOG.md §4 pattern (see GOOG.md:104–106 for the canonical form):

+- `/Users/anton/src/pde-skills/references/html-skeleton.md` — token reference. Patterns must use only the documented tokens (`--accent`, `--ok`, `--danger`, `--warn`, `--info`, `--muted`, `--rule`, `--surface`, `--fg`, `--bg`, and their `-soft` companions). The skeleton already handles dark mode and print.

+- `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.html` lines 115–137 — source of the existing `.axis-card` / `.fm-tag` CSS being promoted into P21/P22. Note: existing CSS uses `var(--border)`, must be re-keyed to `var(--rule)` (the skeleton's authoritative name).
+- `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/GOOG.md` and `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.md` — the canonical content the patterns must match.
-- **The question:** one-sentence framing of the debate
-- **Bull:** the bullish read with specific evidence claims
-- **Bear:** the bearish read with specific evidence claims

-- **Noise vs. structural:** what data is cycle/macro noise vs. what constitutes a durable signal. This is the load-bearing sub-section — it must name a falsifiable observable (e.g., "EUV unit run-rate below 60 for two consecutive years" rather than "EUV demand weakens").

-Keep all 5 axes. Do not adopt GOOG's H3 layer-grouping headers — ASML's debates don't stratify into a clean 4-layer taxonomy the way the AI-disruption debate does. Number them 1–5 as in the current ASML §4 but with the sub-structure inserted.
+One pattern per playbook body section. Detection rules below are summaries; full HTML/CSS specs are designed in the implementation. All shell tokens, all dark-mode-safe by construction.
-Expected size: this section grows from ~750 words to ~1500–1800 words.
-### A2. Frontmatter `failure_modes` + §5 Failure Modes — layer-tag each entry
+- **Detect:** H2 matching `/business architecture|segment mix|revenue mix/i` AND a table whose first column header is `Segment` and second column carries `%` / `share` / `mix`.

+- **Render:** Inline SVG 100%-stacked horizontal bar above the source table. Slice colours cycle through six tokens (`--accent`, `--info`, `--warn`, `--ok`, `--danger`, `--muted`). Slice labels render only on slices ≥8%; the source table below carries the full legend.
+- **Why bar, not donut:** Donuts hide the long tail and don't print well. The bar reads left-to-right, prints in B&W, and the table remains the canonical data source.
+- **Markdown change:** None. Both GOOG.md §1 and ASML.md §1 already match.
-| Customer concentration shock (TSMC + Samsung + Intel) | `[demand layer]` |
-| China export controls broadened to DUV service/spare-parts | `[regulatory layer]` |
-| High-NA cost-per-wafer fails to clear at scale | `[technology layer]` |
-| Reported FCF distorted by customer prepayments | `[accounting layer]` |

+- **Detect (strict, three concurrent conditions):** H2 matching `/(capex|AI investment) cycle|cycle position|normalised margin/i` AND a paragraph containing all of (a) a keyword like `floor / ceiling / base / anchor / sits between`, (b) at least two `%` anchors, (c) within 250 characters of each other.

+- **Render:** Horizontal range strip with gradient track (`--danger-soft → --warn-soft → --ok-soft`), endpoint labels for floor and ceiling, marker tick at the base anchor. Tick position computed from `(base − low) / (high − low)`.

+- **Why strict trigger:** DCF prose is dense with `%` figures that aren't position anchors. Falling through to prose is the correct default; only render the strip when the anchor framing is unambiguous.
+- **Markdown change:** None. GOOG.md §2 ("Normalised-margin anchors") and ASML.md §2 ("Normalised-margin thesis") both match.
-Append the tag at the **end** of each string (matching GOOG's convention — see GOOG.md:12–17 and 146–156). Apply consistently to both the frontmatter list AND the §5 body paragraphs.
-### A3. §2 Capex-Cycle Position — name an explicit evidence-gating condition
+- **Detect:** H2 matching `/catalysts?/i` AND a table with headers matching `Catalyst` + `Probability` + `IV impact`.

+- **Render:** The existing markdown table augmented with two extra cells per row — a blue probability bar and a green/red IV-impact bar (width = midpoint of any range like `−10–20%`, capped at 30% for visual normalisation). Direction column becomes a pill (`--ok-soft / --ok` for positive, `--danger-soft / --danger` for negative, `--warn-soft / --warn` for mixed).
+- **Why inline mini-bars, not scatter:** 8 catalyst points on a scatter lose label legibility; mini-bars preserve identity and table-scan order.

-Add a labelled paragraph (mirroring GOOG.md:77's "AI-capex normalisation signal") naming what would constitute a falsifiable confirmation of cycle-trough-and-re-acceleration. Proposed candidates: SEMI book-to-bill ratio sustained >1.2 for ≥2 quarters; ASML EUV unit shipments crossing 70+ in a quarter; published guidance restoring 2030 LRP envelope without downward revision. Keep brief (one short paragraph) — this is a labelling change, not a content rewrite.
+### P21 — Bull / Bear / Noise-vs-Structural axis grid (§4) — **promotion of existing ASML CSS**
-### A4. Frontmatter `bull_narrative` — already enumerated; keep as-is

+- **Detect:** H2 matching `/sell[- ]side disagreement|disagreement axes/i` AND per-axis H3s where each has a `**The question:**` line followed by `**Bull:**` / `**Bear:**` / `**Noise vs. structural:**` blocks.
+- **Render:** Per-axis card: full-width question banner, two-column Bull/Bear pair (green-soft / red-soft tints), full-width NVS panel beneath (neutral tint). Mobile collapses to single column.

+- **Migration from ASML.html:** Re-key the existing `.axis-card / .bull-block / .bear-block / .nvs` classes to `.p-axis-card / .p-axis-bull / .p-axis-bear / .p-axis-nvs`; swap hardcoded hexes for `--ok-soft / --ok` (bull) and `--danger-soft / --danger` (bear). The hand-authored `<div class="axis-card">` blocks in ASML.html lines 351–467 disappear — replaced by pattern-driven markup.
+- **Markdown change:** None. Both playbooks already use the canonical shape.
-ASML already enumerates (i)–(vi). No change. Listed here so the symmetry pass is explicit.
+### P22 — Layer-tagged failure-mode cards (§5) — **promotion of existing ASML CSS**
+- **Detect:** H2 matching `/failure modes?/i` AND a bullet list where ≥50% of items end with `[xxx layer]` tags.

+- **Render:** Per-mode card with a left-positioned coloured chip carrying the layer label. Auto-generated legend strip above (`aria-hidden`, since each card carries its own tag for screen readers). Fixed palette mapping nine known layers (`interface`, `monetisation`, `cost`, `ecosystem`, `demand`, `regulatory`, `technology`, `accounting`, `FX`) to specific token pairs; unknown layers fall to a muted `-other` style.

+- **Migration from ASML.html:** Re-key existing `.fm / .fm-tag` classes to `.p-fm-card / .p-fm-tag`; bind tag-specific colours to the layer palette. ASML.html's `
…
` block (lines 472–497) is deleted.
+- **Markdown change:** None. All ASML and GOOG failure-mode bullets already end with `[xxx layer]`.
+### P23 — Scenario-axes shape plot (§6) — **reads from frontmatter, not body**
-Rewrite the current paragraph (GOOG.md:10) as an enumerated list of independent upside vectors with (i)/(ii)/(iii)/(iv) inline structure, mirroring ASML.md:10. Proposed vectors:

+- **Detect:** P9 frontmatter parser produces a `scenario_axes` field with bear/bull deltas on `growth_ceiling` and/or `terminal_margin`. The plot lifts into the body immediately under the §6 H2 (matched by `/overrides|rationale/i`).

+- **Render:** Small inline SVG plot — x-axis = growth ceiling (0–25%), y-axis = terminal margin (15–40%), three points (bear / base / bull) connected by a polygon (`fill: --accent-soft`). Reads the shape's asymmetry at a glance: structural-break shape (asymmetric polygon) vs symmetric ±delta shape (isoceles triangle).
+- **Fallback formulas:** When bear/bull overrides are absent, the renderer uses the same `±multiplier` formulas the DCF loader uses (documented in the catalogue entry verbatim so they don't drift).
+- **Markdown change:** None. Both playbooks' frontmatter already populates `scenario_axes`.
-- (i) AI Mode + AI Overview monetise at parity-or-better RPM vs. classical Search
-- (ii) GCP TPU + Vertex stack creates durable economic differentiation; margins expand to high-20s
-- (iii) Waymo monetisation matures (multi-city fleet revenue + licensing)
-- (iv) Cost normalisation: AI capex/revenue ratio declines post-FY2026 to pre-AI levels
-- (v) Distribution moats hold through DOJ remedy phase; default-placement economics preserved in modified form
-- (vi) Gemini consumer adoption produces measurable "first-place-I-ask" share gain
-Keep as a single YAML string (the loader applies it verbatim per SKILL.md spec) — semicolons + inline (i)/(ii) markers, exactly as ASML's bull_narrative is formatted.
+- **Detect:** H2 matching `/historical reset|reset[- ]and[- ]recover|recovery prior/i` AND a table with `Episode | Drawdown | … | Recovery | …` headers.

+- **Render:** Inline SVG scatter on (recovery duration in months × drawdown %). One dot per episode, coloured by severity band (`≤20% → --warn`, `20–40% → --danger`, `>40% → --danger` saturated). Source table sits below as canonical legend.
+- **Why scatter, not horizontal time-bar:** Source data is magnitude × duration, not calendar bounds. Scatter encodes "how deep × how long" directly.
-### G2. §2 AI Investment Cycle Position — add numerical bracketing for terminal-margin choice
+## Slicing — three implementation slices, lowest-risk first

-Currently Path A vs Path B is structurally explicit but lacks numerical anchors. Add a short paragraph (modelled on ASML.md:69's "30% base sits between TTM clean FCF (~24%) and TTM earnings margin (~35%)" pattern) that brackets the 30% base choice numerically — name the TTM-clean SBC-stripped FCF margin, the segment-level Search margin (if disclosed), and the capex-blended group margin. Use approximate ranges where exact figures aren't disclosed; flag any inferred numbers explicitly (e.g., "approximate, derived from segment disclosures").
+The slicing order exercises infrastructure under known-correct conditions before any new visual logic ships, so a regression in one slice doesn't compound into the next.
-### G3. §6 Overrides table — numerical anchor in `terminal_margin` rationale row

+**Slice 1 — promotions only (P21, P22).** Lift ASML's inline CSS into the catalogue and re-key to shell tokens. No new visual logic; the rendering tree is what ASML.html already produces, just driven from markdown. Dark mode starts working everywhere it didn't before, and GOOG.html immediately gains both sections. Verification surface: 2 playbooks × 2 sections = 4 cases, each comparable to the existing ASML.html snapshot.

-The current `terminal_margin` rationale (GOOG.md:166) says "30% sits between Search-segment-implied margin and capex-blended group margin" without numbers. Add the same numerical anchors used in G2 inline in the table cell (e.g., "Search-segment-implied ~X%, capex-blended group ~Y%, base 30% sits between"). Mirrors the precision standard in ASML.md:130.

+**Slice 2 — table-driven inline charts (P18, P20, P24).** Three patterns that decorate existing markdown tables with inline SVG. Common implementation primitive: a numeric-cell parser (`~35%`, `−10–20%`, `~14 months` → numeric value). Build the parser once, then each pattern is ~30 lines of SVG generation. No detection ambiguity — each pattern has a unique header-column signature.

+**Slice 3 — narrative + frontmatter patterns (P19, P23).** P19 has the strictest detection rule in the set (three concurrent conditions) and consumes free-form prose; P23 reads from frontmatter and depends on P9's parser. Both are the highest-risk patterns; saving them for last gets infrastructure confidence first.

+Each slice is one PR / one Linear issue. A new Linear issue should be created per slice under the Equity Skill Pack project, scoped to the patterns it lands plus the re-render of GOOG.html and ASML.html for verification.
## What this plan does NOT change

-- All frontmatter override **values** (base_wacc, growth_ceiling, terminal_margin, confidence_anchor, scenario_axes numerics) — content reviewed and judged appropriately calibrated; no change requested.
-- §1 Business Architecture in both — already symmetric; differing segment counts reflect actual business shape.
-- §3 Active Catalysts in both — already symmetric (8 catalysts each, same column shape).
-- §6 Overrides table beyond G3 — `base_wacc` and `growth_ceiling` rationales are already well-anchored.
-- §7 Historical Reset-and-Recover Priors — already symmetric (3 episodes each + mean-reversion paragraph + structural-break caveat).
-- META.md — explicitly out of scope per user request.
-- HTML files (ASML.html, GOOG.html) — separate re-rendering step, called out as follow-up.

+- **Frontmatter schema and override values.** `base_wacc`, `growth_ceiling`, `terminal_margin`, `confidence_anchor`, `scenario_axes` numerics, and narrative strings are all untouched. P23 reads them; it does not write them.
+- **Playbook markdown bodies.** Zero changes to GOOG.md or ASML.md. All seven detection rules trigger on the current content.
+- **The `/pde-render-html` skill workflow.** Steps 1–7 stay the same. P18–P24 are read as part of Step 4's pattern-table lookup, just like P1–P17 already are.

+- **Existing patterns P1–P17.** No edits to existing pattern entries. P18–P24 sit at the end of the catalogue and inherit the existing first-match-wins ordering. Conflicts checked: P4 (NFR table) needs `Target / SLO / p95 / Latency` headers — none appear in playbook tables. P17 (risks table) needs a `<table>` with `Risk / Severity / Mitigation` columns — playbook failure modes are bullets, never tables. No collisions.

+- **Other consumers of the pattern catalogue.** Pattern names are prefixed `p-` per the catalogue convention; no class-name collisions with the host shell, the existing patterns, or hand-authored content elsewhere.
+- **External data sources.** No new yfinance / EDGAR / web fetches. All visual data lives in the markdown or the frontmatter the markdown already carries.
+After each slice, the following must pass:

-1. **YAML frontmatter still parses.** Quick check by running a Python one-liner that does `yaml.safe_load(open('playbooks/ASML.md').read().split('---')[1])` for both files. Confirm all 10 expected top-level keys present (`ticker`, `last_updated`, `base_wacc`, `growth_ceiling`, `terminal_margin`, `confidence_anchor`, `bear_narrative`, `base_narrative`, `bull_narrative`, `failure_modes`, `scenario_axes`) and that `scenario_axes` deserialises to the expected nested dict.

+1. **Pattern catalogue parses and is consistent.** Each new entry has the six-part structure (Name, Detect, HTML, CSS, Accessibility, When to use / not). All CSS uses only documented skeleton tokens. No hex literals in pattern CSS. Verify by grepping the appended block for `#[0-9a-f]{3,6}` and confirming zero matches.
+2. **Self-containment preserved.** Re-rendered GOOG.html and ASML.html have zero ``, zero `

+3. **Symmetry test.** Re-render both playbooks. Open side-by-side. The patterns landed in the current slice produce visually equivalent treatment in both files. For Slice 1 specifically: GOOG.html now has axis cards and failure-mode cards that visually match ASML.html (modulo content differences).

+4. **Detection precision.** No pattern fires on unrelated H2/table content. Spot-check by running each pattern's detect regex against the other playbook's sections and any other markdown the team has (DESIGN.md, CHARTER.md, docs/roadmap.md) — none should match.

+5. **Dark mode + print.** Open re-rendered files with `prefers-color-scheme: dark` (DevTools rendering panel). All new patterns invert correctly. Print preview shows no black-on-black or white-on-white.

+6. **Accessibility.** Every inline SVG has `role="img"` and a populated `aria-label`. Every chart pattern has a co-located source `<table>` or paragraph that carries the canonical data. Run an axe/lighthouse audit if available.

+7. **Future-ticker test.** Create a 30-line scratch playbook stub with the standard 7-section structure and minimal content (one segment, one catalyst, one axis, one failure mode, one episode). Render it. All seven patterns should activate. This is the gold-standard test that the patterns are reusable, not just GOOG/ASML-specific.
+8. **No regressions in existing patterns.** Re-render an existing non-playbook doc that exercises P1–P17 (e.g. one of the pde-skills design docs). Visual output identical to its previous render.

-2. **No frontmatter override values changed.** `git diff playbooks/ASML.md playbooks/GOOG.md` filtered to the YAML region: confirm `base_wacc`, `growth_ceiling`, `terminal_margin`, `confidence_anchor`, `scenario_axes` numerics are byte-identical to pre-edit. Narrative strings and `failure_modes` strings are the only frontmatter changes expected.
-   - §4 in both has identical sub-section structure (5 axes in ASML × 4 layers + Cloud in GOOG; each axis has Question / Bull / Bear / Noise-vs-Structural)
-   - §5 + frontmatter failure_modes in both carry layer tags at end of each entry
-   - §2 in both names an explicit evidence-gating condition
-   - Bull narrative in both enumerates upside vectors with (i)/(ii)/… inline markers

-4. **End-to-end: skill still loads both playbooks cleanly.** Run `/stock-screen ASML` then `/stock-signal ASML` then `/stock-model ASML` and confirm the `Playbook loaded: playbooks/ASML.md` line appears with all expected overrides listed; repeat for GOOG. (Note: model can be invoked with cached signal output to avoid hitting yfinance MCP; the goal is to verify the loader, not re-run the full DCF.)

-5. **Word-count sanity.** Expected: ASML grows from ~3200 to ~4200 words (~30% increase, concentrated in §4). GOOG grows from ~4400 to ~4600 words (~5% increase, spread across frontmatter bull_narrative + §2 + §6). If either delta is materially outside this range, the rewrite has likely drifted into scope creep — re-scope before committing.
## Follow-up (out of scope for this plan)
-- Re-render `playbooks/ASML.html` and `playbooks/GOOG.html` via the global `pde-render-html` skill. User explicitly framed this as a later step.
-- Consider whether META.md should be brought up to the same bar in a follow-up pass.
-- Consider extracting the playbook structure into a written template under `docs/` so future tickers (NFLX, AMZN, etc.) start at the higher bar by default.

+- **Linear issues.** Create three Linear issues under the Equity Skill Pack project (ABA team), one per slice, attached to the current cycle. Title pattern: "Playbook patterns slice N — P21+P22 / P18+P20+P24 / P19+P23". Update each to In Progress when the slice starts, Done only after the slice is committed AND pushed.

+- **Playbook authoring conventions.** Once the patterns land, document the markdown conventions that activate them as a short section in `playbooks/README.md` (create if absent) so a future agent authoring a new ticker playbook gets the visual treatment by writing the standard shape.

+- **META playbook upgrade.** META.md currently does not match the canonical structure. After P18–P24 are catalogued, META can be brought up to the same bar with the same zero-markdown-change benefit — but this is a separate plan.

+- **Per-skill annotations.** Once P18–P24 are stable, the `/pde-render-html` skill's "Workflow Step 4" could optionally reference a "playbook profile" so the skill author knows which patterns target this artefact type. Not load-bearing; a UX nicety.

+- **Pattern P17 trigger relaxation.** P17 currently requires the failure-modes content to be a markdown table. After P22 lands, P17 stays as-is — but if a future playbook author writes a Risk/Severity/Mitigation table inside the §5 section, both could attempt to fire. Pattern ordering (P17 before P22) gives P17 priority, which is the right behaviour. Worth documenting explicitly in the P22 catalogue entry.

input.plan:
# Plan — Helpful visuals for GOOG and ASML playbooks (patterns P18–P24) ## Context The two playbooks render asymmetrically today: - `playbooks/GOOG.html` falls through to prose fallback for all seven body sections. Only the global patterns P9 (frontmatter → meta-grid), P10 (status pills), and P4 (numeric tables) are active. - `playbooks/ASML.html` uses the same global patterns, but additionally carries **hand-authored inline CSS** for §4 (`.axis-card` / `.bull-block` / `.bear-block` / `.nvs`, lines 115–128) and §5 (`.fm` / `.fm-tag`, lines 131–132). These don't live in the pattern catalogue, so they don't transfer to other tickers and they don't follow the skeleton's dark-mode token scheme. Both observations are symptoms of the same problem: the playbook body has rich, structured content (segment mixes, margin anchors, catalyst probabilities, bull/bear/noise triplets, layer-tagged failure modes, scenario_axes asymmetry, drawdown/recovery episodes), but no patterns in the catalogue target that content. So either a reader gets prose or a future agent has to reinvent custom HTML, and the two playbooks drift apart visually each time someone re-renders them. The goal is to add seven new patterns (P18–P24) to `references/html-patterns.md`, one per body section, so that: - Every playbook body section gets a visual treatment without per-ticker custom HTML. - ASML's existing `.axis-card` / `.fm-tag` CSS is promoted into the catalogue (re-keyed to shell tokens, so dark mode works) and GOOG inherits both treatments automatically on the next render. - All data flows from playbook markdown + frontmatter only. No external lookups, no MCP calls, no signal-output reads. SVGs are inline. - New patterns activate on the current GOOG.md / ASML.md content with **zero markdown changes** — they read the shape that's already there. - Future tickers (NFLX, AMZN, META rewrite) get the same visual treatment automatically as long as their markdown follows the playbook conventions. ## Files modified **Primary edit:** - `/Users/anton/src/pde-skills/references/html-patterns.md` — append seven new pattern specs (P18 through P24) following the existing six-part structure (Name, Detect, HTML, CSS, Accessibility, When to use / when not to use). **Re-rendered (verification, not edits):** - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/GOOG.html` — re-render via `/pde-render-html playbooks/GOOG.md` after each slice lands; expect the symmetry gap with ASML to close visibly. - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.html` — re-render via `/pde-render-html playbooks/ASML.md`; expect the hand-authored inline CSS to be replaced by pattern-driven markup. The visual surface should stay near-identical to the existing ASML.html, just driven from markdown. **Read-only references:** - `/Users/anton/src/pde-skills/references/html-skeleton.md` — token reference. Patterns must use only the documented tokens (`--accent`, `--ok`, `--danger`, `--warn`, `--info`, `--muted`, `--rule`, `--surface`, `--fg`, `--bg`, and their `-soft` companions). The skeleton already handles dark mode and print. - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.html` lines 115–137 — source of the existing `.axis-card` / `.fm-tag` CSS being promoted into P21/P22. Note: existing CSS uses `var(--border)`, must be re-keyed to `var(--rule)` (the skeleton's authoritative name). - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/GOOG.md` and `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.md` — the canonical content the patterns must match. ## The seven new patterns One pattern per playbook body section. Detection rules below are summaries; full HTML/CSS specs are designed in the implementation. All shell tokens, all dark-mode-safe by construction. ### P18 — Segment-mix horizontal stack bar (§1) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/business architecture|segment mix|revenue mix/i` AND a table whose first column header is `Segment` and second column carries `%` / `share` / `mix`. - **Render:** Inline SVG 100%-stacked horizontal bar above the source table. Slice colours cycle through six tokens (`--accent`, `--info`, `--warn`, `--ok`, `--danger`, `--muted`). Slice labels render only on slices ≥8%; the source table below carries the full legend. - **Why bar, not donut:** Donuts hide the long tail and don't print well. The bar reads left-to-right, prints in B&W, and the table remains the canonical data source. - **Markdown change:** None. Both GOOG.md §1 and ASML.md §1 already match. ### P19 — Margin position-band strip (§2) - **Detect (strict, three concurrent conditions):** H2 matching `/(capex|AI investment) cycle|cycle position|normalised margin/i` AND a paragraph containing all of (a) a keyword like `floor / ceiling / base / anchor / sits between`, (b) at least two `%` anchors, (c) within 250 characters of each other. - **Render:** Horizontal range strip with gradient track (`--danger-soft → --warn-soft → --ok-soft`), endpoint labels for floor and ceiling, marker tick at the base anchor. Tick position computed from `(base − low) / (high − low)`. - **Why strict trigger:** DCF prose is dense with `%` figures that aren't position anchors. Falling through to prose is the correct default; only render the strip when the anchor framing is unambiguous. - **Markdown change:** None. GOOG.md §2 ("Normalised-margin anchors") and ASML.md §2 ("Normalised-margin thesis") both match. ### P20 — Catalyst probability × IV-impact mini-bars (§3) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/catalysts?/i` AND a table with headers matching `Catalyst` + `Probability` + `IV impact`. - **Render:** The existing markdown table augmented with two extra cells per row — a blue probability bar and a green/red IV-impact bar (width = midpoint of any range like `−10–20%`, capped at 30% for visual normalisation). Direction column becomes a pill (`--ok-soft / --ok` for positive, `--danger-soft / --danger` for negative, `--warn-soft / --warn` for mixed). - **Why inline mini-bars, not scatter:** 8 catalyst points on a scatter lose label legibility; mini-bars preserve identity and table-scan order. - **Markdown change:** None. Both §3 tables match. ### P21 — Bull / Bear / Noise-vs-Structural axis grid (§4) — **promotion of existing ASML CSS** - **Detect:** H2 matching `/sell[- ]side disagreement|disagreement axes/i` AND per-axis H3s where each has a `**The question:**` line followed by `**Bull:**` / `**Bear:**` / `**Noise vs. structural:**` blocks. - **Render:** Per-axis card: full-width question banner, two-column Bull/Bear pair (green-soft / red-soft tints), full-width NVS panel beneath (neutral tint). Mobile collapses to single column. - **Migration from ASML.html:** Re-key the existing `.axis-card / .bull-block / .bear-block / .nvs` classes to `.p-axis-card / .p-axis-bull / .p-axis-bear / .p-axis-nvs`; swap hardcoded hexes for `--ok-soft / --ok` (bull) and `--danger-soft / --danger` (bear). The hand-authored `
` blocks in ASML.html lines 351–467 disappear — replaced by pattern-driven markup. - **Markdown change:** None. Both playbooks already use the canonical shape. ### P22 — Layer-tagged failure-mode cards (§5) — **promotion of existing ASML CSS** - **Detect:** H2 matching `/failure modes?/i` AND a bullet list where ≥50% of items end with `[xxx layer]` tags. - **Render:** Per-mode card with a left-positioned coloured chip carrying the layer label. Auto-generated legend strip above (`aria-hidden`, since each card carries its own tag for screen readers). Fixed palette mapping nine known layers (`interface`, `monetisation`, `cost`, `ecosystem`, `demand`, `regulatory`, `technology`, `accounting`, `FX`) to specific token pairs; unknown layers fall to a muted `-other` style. - **Migration from ASML.html:** Re-key existing `.fm / .fm-tag` classes to `.p-fm-card / .p-fm-tag`; bind tag-specific colours to the layer palette. ASML.html's `
…
` block (lines 472–497) is deleted. - **Markdown change:** None. All ASML and GOOG failure-mode bullets already end with `[xxx layer]`. ### P23 — Scenario-axes shape plot (§6) — **reads from frontmatter, not body** - **Detect:** P9 frontmatter parser produces a `scenario_axes` field with bear/bull deltas on `growth_ceiling` and/or `terminal_margin`. The plot lifts into the body immediately under the §6 H2 (matched by `/overrides|rationale/i`). - **Render:** Small inline SVG plot — x-axis = growth ceiling (0–25%), y-axis = terminal margin (15–40%), three points (bear / base / bull) connected by a polygon (`fill: --accent-soft`). Reads the shape's asymmetry at a glance: structural-break shape (asymmetric polygon) vs symmetric ±delta shape (isoceles triangle). - **Fallback formulas:** When bear/bull overrides are absent, the renderer uses the same `±multiplier` formulas the DCF loader uses (documented in the catalogue entry verbatim so they don't drift). - **Markdown change:** None. Both playbooks' frontmatter already populates `scenario_axes`. ### P24 — Reset-and-recover scatter (§7) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/historical reset|reset[- ]and[- ]recover|recovery prior/i` AND a table with `Episode | Drawdown | … | Recovery | …` headers. - **Render:** Inline SVG scatter on (recovery duration in months × drawdown %). One dot per episode, coloured by severity band (`≤20% → --warn`, `20–40% → --danger`, `>40% → --danger` saturated). Source table sits below as canonical legend. - **Why scatter, not horizontal time-bar:** Source data is magnitude × duration, not calendar bounds. Scatter encodes "how deep × how long" directly. - **Markdown change:** None. Both §7 tables match. ## Slicing — three implementation slices, lowest-risk first The slicing order exercises infrastructure under known-correct conditions before any new visual logic ships, so a regression in one slice doesn't compound into the next. **Slice 1 — promotions only (P21, P22).** Lift ASML's inline CSS into the catalogue and re-key to shell tokens. No new visual logic; the rendering tree is what ASML.html already produces, just driven from markdown. Dark mode starts working everywhere it didn't before, and GOOG.html immediately gains both sections. Verification surface: 2 playbooks × 2 sections = 4 cases, each comparable to the existing ASML.html snapshot. **Slice 2 — table-driven inline charts (P18, P20, P24).** Three patterns that decorate existing markdown tables with inline SVG. Common implementation primitive: a numeric-cell parser (`~35%`, `−10–20%`, `~14 months` → numeric value). Build the parser once, then each pattern is ~30 lines of SVG generation. No detection ambiguity — each pattern has a unique header-column signature. **Slice 3 — narrative + frontmatter patterns (P19, P23).** P19 has the strictest detection rule in the set (three concurrent conditions) and consumes free-form prose; P23 reads from frontmatter and depends on P9's parser. Both are the highest-risk patterns; saving them for last gets infrastructure confidence first. Each slice is one PR / one Linear issue. A new Linear issue should be created per slice under the Equity Skill Pack project, scoped to the patterns it lands plus the re-render of GOOG.html and ASML.html for verification. ## What this plan does NOT change - **Frontmatter schema and override values.** `base_wacc`, `growth_ceiling`, `terminal_margin`, `confidence_anchor`, `scenario_axes` numerics, and narrative strings are all untouched. P23 reads them; it does not write them. - **Playbook markdown bodies.** Zero changes to GOOG.md or ASML.md. All seven detection rules trigger on the current content. - **The `/pde-render-html` skill workflow.** Steps 1–7 stay the same. P18–P24 are read as part of Step 4's pattern-table lookup, just like P1–P17 already are. - **Existing patterns P1–P17.** No edits to existing pattern entries. P18–P24 sit at the end of the catalogue and inherit the existing first-match-wins ordering. Conflicts checked: P4 (NFR table) needs `Target / SLO / p95 / Latency` headers — none appear in playbook tables. P17 (risks table) needs a `` with `Risk / Severity / Mitigation` columns — playbook failure modes are bullets, never tables. No collisions. - **Other consumers of the pattern catalogue.** Pattern names are prefixed `p-` per the catalogue convention; no class-name collisions with the host shell, the existing patterns, or hand-authored content elsewhere. - **External data sources.** No new yfinance / EDGAR / web fetches. All visual data lives in the markdown or the frontmatter the markdown already carries. ## Verification After each slice, the following must pass: 1. **Pattern catalogue parses and is consistent.** Each new entry has the six-part structure (Name, Detect, HTML, CSS, Accessibility, When to use / not). All CSS uses only documented skeleton tokens. No hex literals in pattern CSS. Verify by grepping the appended block for `#[0-9a-f]{3,6}` and confirming zero matches. 2. **Self-containment preserved.** Re-rendered GOOG.html and ASML.html have zero ``, zero `

# Plan — Helpful visuals for GOOG and ASML playbooks (patterns P18–P24) ## Context The two playbooks render asymmetrically today: - `playbooks/GOOG.html` falls through to prose fallback for all seven body sections. Only the global patterns P9 (frontmatter → meta-grid), P10 (status pills), and P4 (numeric tables) are active. - `playbooks/ASML.html` uses the same global patterns, but additionally carries **hand-authored inline CSS** for §4 (`.axis-card` / `.bull-block` / `.bear-block` / `.nvs`, lines 115–128) and §5 (`.fm` / `.fm-tag`, lines 131–132). These don't live in the pattern catalogue, so they don't transfer to other tickers and they don't follow the skeleton's dark-mode token scheme. Both observations are symptoms of the same problem: the playbook body has rich, structured content (segment mixes, margin anchors, catalyst probabilities, bull/bear/noise triplets, layer-tagged failure modes, scenario_axes asymmetry, drawdown/recovery episodes), but no patterns in the catalogue target that content. So either a reader gets prose or a future agent has to reinvent custom HTML, and the two playbooks drift apart visually each time someone re-renders them. The goal is to add seven new patterns (P18–P24) to `references/html-patterns.md`, one per body section, so that: - Every playbook body section gets a visual treatment without per-ticker custom HTML. - ASML's existing `.axis-card` / `.fm-tag` CSS is promoted into the catalogue (re-keyed to shell tokens, so dark mode works) and GOOG inherits both treatments automatically on the next render. - All data flows from playbook markdown + frontmatter only. No external lookups, no MCP calls, no signal-output reads. SVGs are inline. - New patterns activate on the current GOOG.md / ASML.md content with **zero markdown changes** — they read the shape that's already there. - Future tickers (NFLX, AMZN, META rewrite) get the same visual treatment automatically as long as their markdown follows the playbook conventions. ## Files modified **Primary edit:** - `/Users/anton/src/pde-skills/references/html-patterns.md` — append seven new pattern specs (P18 through P24) following the existing six-part structure (Name, Detect, HTML, CSS, Accessibility, When to use / when not to use). **Re-rendered (verification, not edits):** - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/GOOG.html` — re-render via `/pde-render-html playbooks/GOOG.md` after each slice lands; expect the symmetry gap with ASML to close visibly. - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.html` — re-render via `/pde-render-html playbooks/ASML.md`; expect the hand-authored inline CSS to be replaced by pattern-driven markup. The visual surface should stay near-identical to the existing ASML.html, just driven from markdown. **Read-only references:** - `/Users/anton/src/pde-skills/references/html-skeleton.md` — token reference. Patterns must use only the documented tokens (`--accent`, `--ok`, `--danger`, `--warn`, `--info`, `--muted`, `--rule`, `--surface`, `--fg`, `--bg`, and their `-soft` companions). The skeleton already handles dark mode and print. - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.html` lines 115–137 — source of the existing `.axis-card` / `.fm-tag` CSS being promoted into P21/P22. Note: existing CSS uses `var(--border)`, must be re-keyed to `var(--rule)` (the skeleton's authoritative name). - `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/GOOG.md` and `/Users/anton/src/stock-review/playbooks/ASML.md` — the canonical content the patterns must match. ## The seven new patterns One pattern per playbook body section. Detection rules below are summaries; full HTML/CSS specs are designed in the implementation. All shell tokens, all dark-mode-safe by construction. ### P18 — Segment-mix horizontal stack bar (§1) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/business architecture|segment mix|revenue mix/i` AND a table whose first column header is `Segment` and second column carries `%` / `share` / `mix`. - **Render:** Inline SVG 100%-stacked horizontal bar above the source table. Slice colours cycle through six tokens (`--accent`, `--info`, `--warn`, `--ok`, `--danger`, `--muted`). Slice labels render only on slices ≥8%; the source table below carries the full legend. - **Why bar, not donut:** Donuts hide the long tail and don't print well. The bar reads left-to-right, prints in B&W, and the table remains the canonical data source. - **Markdown change:** None. Both GOOG.md §1 and ASML.md §1 already match. ### P19 — Margin position-band strip (§2) - **Detect (strict, three concurrent conditions):** H2 matching `/(capex|AI investment) cycle|cycle position|normalised margin/i` AND a paragraph containing all of (a) a keyword like `floor / ceiling / base / anchor / sits between`, (b) at least two `%` anchors, (c) within 250 characters of each other. - **Render:** Horizontal range strip with gradient track (`--danger-soft → --warn-soft → --ok-soft`), endpoint labels for floor and ceiling, marker tick at the base anchor. Tick position computed from `(base − low) / (high − low)`. - **Why strict trigger:** DCF prose is dense with `%` figures that aren't position anchors. Falling through to prose is the correct default; only render the strip when the anchor framing is unambiguous. - **Markdown change:** None. GOOG.md §2 ("Normalised-margin anchors") and ASML.md §2 ("Normalised-margin thesis") both match. ### P20 — Catalyst probability × IV-impact mini-bars (§3) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/catalysts?/i` AND a table with headers matching `Catalyst` + `Probability` + `IV impact`. - **Render:** The existing markdown table augmented with two extra cells per row — a blue probability bar and a green/red IV-impact bar (width = midpoint of any range like `−10–20%`, capped at 30% for visual normalisation). Direction column becomes a pill (`--ok-soft / --ok` for positive, `--danger-soft / --danger` for negative, `--warn-soft / --warn` for mixed). - **Why inline mini-bars, not scatter:** 8 catalyst points on a scatter lose label legibility; mini-bars preserve identity and table-scan order. - **Markdown change:** None. Both §3 tables match. ### P21 — Bull / Bear / Noise-vs-Structural axis grid (§4) — **promotion of existing ASML CSS** - **Detect:** H2 matching `/sell[- ]side disagreement|disagreement axes/i` AND per-axis H3s where each has a `**The question:**` line followed by `**Bull:**` / `**Bear:**` / `**Noise vs. structural:**` blocks. - **Render:** Per-axis card: full-width question banner, two-column Bull/Bear pair (green-soft / red-soft tints), full-width NVS panel beneath (neutral tint). Mobile collapses to single column. - **Migration from ASML.html:** Re-key the existing `.axis-card / .bull-block / .bear-block / .nvs` classes to `.p-axis-card / .p-axis-bull / .p-axis-bear / .p-axis-nvs`; swap hardcoded hexes for `--ok-soft / --ok` (bull) and `--danger-soft / --danger` (bear). The hand-authored `
` blocks in ASML.html lines 351–467 disappear — replaced by pattern-driven markup. - **Markdown change:** None. Both playbooks already use the canonical shape. ### P22 — Layer-tagged failure-mode cards (§5) — **promotion of existing ASML CSS** - **Detect:** H2 matching `/failure modes?/i` AND a bullet list where ≥50% of items end with `[xxx layer]` tags. - **Render:** Per-mode card with a left-positioned coloured chip carrying the layer label. Auto-generated legend strip above (`aria-hidden`, since each card carries its own tag for screen readers). Fixed palette mapping nine known layers (`interface`, `monetisation`, `cost`, `ecosystem`, `demand`, `regulatory`, `technology`, `accounting`, `FX`) to specific token pairs; unknown layers fall to a muted `-other` style. - **Migration from ASML.html:** Re-key existing `.fm / .fm-tag` classes to `.p-fm-card / .p-fm-tag`; bind tag-specific colours to the layer palette. ASML.html's `
…
` block (lines 472–497) is deleted. - **Markdown change:** None. All ASML and GOOG failure-mode bullets already end with `[xxx layer]`. ### P23 — Scenario-axes shape plot (§6) — **reads from frontmatter, not body** - **Detect:** P9 frontmatter parser produces a `scenario_axes` field with bear/bull deltas on `growth_ceiling` and/or `terminal_margin`. The plot lifts into the body immediately under the §6 H2 (matched by `/overrides|rationale/i`). - **Render:** Small inline SVG plot — x-axis = growth ceiling (0–25%), y-axis = terminal margin (15–40%), three points (bear / base / bull) connected by a polygon (`fill: --accent-soft`). Reads the shape's asymmetry at a glance: structural-break shape (asymmetric polygon) vs symmetric ±delta shape (isoceles triangle). - **Fallback formulas:** When bear/bull overrides are absent, the renderer uses the same `±multiplier` formulas the DCF loader uses (documented in the catalogue entry verbatim so they don't drift). - **Markdown change:** None. Both playbooks' frontmatter already populates `scenario_axes`. ### P24 — Reset-and-recover scatter (§7) - **Detect:** H2 matching `/historical reset|reset[- ]and[- ]recover|recovery prior/i` AND a table with `Episode | Drawdown | … | Recovery | …` headers. - **Render:** Inline SVG scatter on (recovery duration in months × drawdown %). One dot per episode, coloured by severity band (`≤20% → --warn`, `20–40% → --danger`, `>40% → --danger` saturated). Source table sits below as canonical legend. - **Why scatter, not horizontal time-bar:** Source data is magnitude × duration, not calendar bounds. Scatter encodes "how deep × how long" directly. - **Markdown change:** None. Both §7 tables match. ## Slicing — three implementation slices, lowest-risk first The slicing order exercises infrastructure under known-correct conditions before any new visual logic ships, so a regression in one slice doesn't compound into the next. **Slice 1 — promotions only (P21, P22).** Lift ASML's inline CSS into the catalogue and re-key to shell tokens. No new visual logic; the rendering tree is what ASML.html already produces, just driven from markdown. Dark mode starts working everywhere it didn't before, and GOOG.html immediately gains both sections. Verification surface: 2 playbooks × 2 sections = 4 cases, each comparable to the existing ASML.html snapshot. **Slice 2 — table-driven inline charts (P18, P20, P24).** Three patterns that decorate existing markdown tables with inline SVG. Common implementation primitive: a numeric-cell parser (`~35%`, `−10–20%`, `~14 months` → numeric value). Build the parser once, then each pattern is ~30 lines of SVG generation. No detection ambiguity — each pattern has a unique header-column signature. **Slice 3 — narrative + frontmatter patterns (P19, P23).** P19 has the strictest detection rule in the set (three concurrent conditions) and consumes free-form prose; P23 reads from frontmatter and depends on P9's parser. Both are the highest-risk patterns; saving them for last gets infrastructure confidence first. Each slice is one PR / one Linear issue. A new Linear issue should be created per slice under the Equity Skill Pack project, scoped to the patterns it lands plus the re-render of GOOG.html and ASML.html for verification. ## What this plan does NOT change - **Frontmatter schema and override values.** `base_wacc`, `growth_ceiling`, `terminal_margin`, `confidence_anchor`, `scenario_axes` numerics, and narrative strings are all untouched. P23 reads them; it does not write them. - **Playbook markdown bodies.** Zero changes to GOOG.md or ASML.md. P18–P24 are read as part of Step 4's pattern-table lookup, just like P1–P17 already are. - **Existing patterns P1–P17.** No edits to existing pattern entries. # AGENTS.md

Instructions for any coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, etc.) working in this repository. Tool-agnostic by design — see `CLAUDE.md` for any Claude-Code-specific additions.

## What this repo is

A personal equity research skill-pack for a tech-focused investor. See `CHARTER.md` for what this pack is for and which boundaries are locked, and `DESIGN.md` for the architectural reference.

Tasks and milestones live in Linear: https://linear.app/ababushkin/project/equity-skill-pack-b8446cbaab6b/overview

## Operating principle

Depth on a curated set of specialist-supported tickers — see `CHARTER.md`, `COVERAGE.md`, and `docs/roadmap.md` for current scope and priorities.

## Build tools by component

| Component | Build with |
|-----------|-----------|
| yfinance MCP server | `/pde:design-doc` → `agent-skills:build` |
| EDGAR MCP server | `/pde:design-doc` → `agent-skills:build` |
| `/stock-signal`, `/stock-screen`, `/stock-timing`, `/stock-model`, `/stock-equity` skills | `skill-creator:skill-creator` |
| Report UI (Vite + React) | `agent-skills:build` |

## Permissions

Per-agent local settings pre-allow `rtk ls *` and `rtk read *` — use these instead of bare `ls`/`cat`.

- Claude Code: `.claude/settings.local.json`
- Codex: `.codex/settings.local.json`

## Linear workflow

Linear is authoritative for status. Local task lists are fine for within-session bookkeeping; they don't replace a Linear issue.

**Project:** Equity skill pack — https://linear.app/ababushkin/project/equity-skill-pack-b8446cbaab6b/overview (team ABA / Personal).

**Cycles.** Work is planned across cycles, often spanning multiple projects at once. When picking up an issue, prefer ones already in the current cycle. If you start something not in the cycle, decide explicitly whether to pull it in or defer — don't silently expand cycle scope. Use `mcp__linear-server__list_cycles` to see the current cycle.

**On start of any issue:**
- Move to **In Progress** via `mcp__linear-server__save_issue`.
- If the issue isn't yet in the current cycle and you intend to ship it this cycle, assign it to the current cycle.

**On completion:**
- Move to **Done** only after the work is committed AND pushed. An issue isn't Done if the work only exists on a local branch.
- Status updates happen at the moment of state change — not batched at end of session.

**Blocked** = leave In Progress + add a blocker comment naming the blocker. Don't silently park work.

**New work surfaced mid-flight** becomes a new Linear issue, slotted into a cycle deliberately. Don't silently expand scope.

## Git

Do not add `Co-Authored-By` trailers to commit messages.

# Engineering principles — agentic (tier 2)

These are the agent-specific engineering principles for the pde-skills pack. "Agent-specific" means: these principles only have force, or only make sense, because the implementer is an AI agent rather than a human. They sit on top of the universal tier (`eng-principles-universal.md`) — they don't replace it.

The test for tier 2: would this principle survive a counterfactual world without AI coding agents? If no, it belongs here. If yes, it belongs in the universal file. Some universal principles (small batches, boring technology, review) bite harder under agents than under humans alone — those are noted at the end as amplifications, not duplicated as new principles.

Skills, hooks, and sub-agent personas trace to principles in either tier; some trace to both. Where a tier-2 principle has a tier-1 ancestor (e.g. agentic Principle 4 amplifies universal Principle 1), the relationship is named.

---

## Part 1 — Guiding principles

These are the seven beliefs specific to agentic engineering. If a decision conflicts with a principle, the principle wins by default — and the burden of justification sits with the person arguing to deviate.

### 1. Context is the asset; loading it is the work

What's in the agent's context window determines output quality more than the underlying model's capability. Anthropic's own guidance puts it bluntly: every new token introduced depletes the agent's attention budget, and the right framing is "find the smallest set of high-signal tokens that maximise the likelihood of your desired outcome." Loading the right files, conventions, prior decisions, and constraints is engineering — not a preamble to engineering. An agent given the wrong context produces wrong work confidently. An agent given a thin slice of the right context produces narrow but accurate work.

**Implication:** Every skill names the context it expects loaded before the work begins — files, principles, prior decisions, conventions. "The agent will figure out what to read" is the same anti-pattern as "we'll figure out the spec in the code" from universal Principle 2.

### 2. Hallucination is the default; sources are the brake

Agents fabricate APIs, library behaviour, and version-specific quirks with high confidence. The fabrications look plausible because they're statistically reasonable continuations of training data — they're just wrong. Treating agent-produced code as trustworthy by default loses time the moment the wrong API call hits production. Every framework or library claim is verified against current docs or actual runtime behaviour, and inferences are flagged as inferences. This is the engineering analogue of universal Principle 4 (name your assumptions). The difference is that agents will assert assumptions as facts unless the skill explicitly forces a citation.

**Implication:** Skills that touch framework or library APIs require source citations — links to current docs, function signatures verified against the actual library version. Unverified claims are flagged in the work product, not silently shipped.

### 3. The shortest path is the failure mode; the spec is the seatbelt

Without an explicit problem statement, an agent optimises for "produce something that compiles," not "solve the stated problem." The agent has no stake in the original problem and no memory of the conversation that produced it; it has only what's in context now, plus a strong gradient toward producing output. Specs aren't ceremony — they're the only thing holding the work to the actual task. This isn't true of human engineers in the same way; humans carry implicit understanding from the conversation, the channel, the team's history. Agents do not.

**Implication:** Every implementation skill restates the problem at the start of its workflow, before any code is written. Skills explicitly forbid jumping to solution language until the problem statement is acknowledged. The restatement is how the agent's first output anchors to the actual task rather than to "what would compile."

### 4. Evidence beats vibes; "seems right" is never sufficient

Agents will report success based on syntax. "It compiled," "tests ran," "no errors logged" are not the same as "the behaviour the user wanted is now true." Every completion claim demands observable evidence: specific test names that passed, specific log lines that confirm, specific output values that match the expected. This is universal Principle 1 ("shipped is not done; observed is done") applied to the agent's own claims about its work, not just to deployments. The failure mode is the same — declaring victory before observing the outcome — but the trigger is different: agents declare victory at green tests, where humans usually wait for some additional signal.

**Implication:** Skills require evidence, not assertions, in completion claims. "The function works" is rejected; "test_user_creation_with_valid_email passed; log shows user_id 42 written to users table" is accepted. The hook `evidence-claim` (Next) enforces this mechanically.

### 5. The rationalisations are predictable; pre-rebut them

"I'll add tests later," "this is a quick fix so I'll skip the spec," "the existing code does it this way so I won't change it," "the type system is too strict, let me cast to any" — these patterns are in training data and the agent will reach for them when the work gets hard. They are not novel rationalisations the agent invents; they are the catalogue of excuses humans have produced in code review for thirty years, now reflected back. Skills that don't pre-rebut them get bypassed at exactly the moments they were most needed.

**Implication:** Every skill includes a "Common rationalisations" section — a table of typical excuses with documented counter-arguments. This isn't decoration: it's the operational form of the principle. If the skill doesn't name the rationalisation, the agent will use it.

### 6. Stop the line on first failure; never silence the signal

Failing tests get changed, type errors get `any`'d, lint failures get suppressed, assertions get deleted. The default response to a failing signal is investigation, not silencing. This deserves its own principle because the failure mode is so consistent: agents are optimised to make errors disappear, and silencing achieves that faster than fixing. The line between "fix the test that's wrong" and "delete the test that's correct" is a judgement call humans make routinely; agents collapse it almost every time without a hard rule.

**Implication:** Casts to `any`, `// @ts-ignore`, `.skip`, suppression of lint warnings, and deletion of assertions are flagged at the PR level by the `stop-the-line` hook (Now). When the hook fires, the agent's work is sent back for investigation, not for re-suppression.

### 7. Memory lives in artefacts, not in agents

Agents have no continuity between sessions. The ADR, the design doc, the postmortem, the README are how reasoning persists. Doc drift is fatal in agentic contexts in a way it isn't in human-only ones, because there's no human carrying the implicit knowledge from one session to the next. When the next agent reads the codebase, what it sees is the artefacts; what it doesn't see is anything not written down. Rule D3 in eng-principles-universal.md says ADRs are living documents that get superseded rather than ignored. In agentic contexts, that rule is load-bearing — silent drift means the next agent has the wrong model and confidently produces wrong work from it.

**Implication:** Every architectural change updates or supersedes its corresponding ADR or design doc in the same PR. The hook `artefact-pair` (Later) flags code changes without doc updates. Skills that produce design artefacts treat the artefact as the deliverable, not the code.

### 8. Effort is measured in slices and gates, not in calendar time

Days, weeks, and months are human-engineering units — they describe how long a person will sit at a keyboard. Agents don't experience work in those units, and asking an agent to produce "time estimates" forces fabrication: the number is either copied from training-data patterns or hallucinated to look plausible. Worse, it imports a false frame — that the relevant question is "how long will this take" rather than "how many independently verifiable steps does this break into, and what gates separate them." The universal tier inherits Larson's "more than four weeks" trigger, Singer's "six-week appetite," and Nygard's "rollback time estimate" because they were written for human teams. Under agents, the load-bearing unit is the **slice** (a thin vertical change that runs end-to-end and is independently verifiable, per universal Rule B2 and `planning-and-task-breakdown`), and the load-bearing checkpoint is the **gate** (a proceed/stop condition with explicit pass criteria). Appetite survives the translation, but only as a cap expressed in slices, not in weeks.

**Implication:** Skills, doc templates, and ADRs that prompt for "time estimates," "deadlines," "weeks," or "days" are rewritten to prompt for slice counts, gates, milestones, or verification conditions. Where a universal-tier rule names a time unit (Rule A1's "four weeks," Rule C5's "spike time-box," Rule A6's "rollback time estimate"), the agentic translation is: number of slices for A1, number of investigation steps with a stop-the-line condition for C5, ordered rollback steps with a verification gate for A6. Calendar time is permitted only at the human-interface seam — date commitments to stakeholders (universal Rule C2) — not inside the work itself.

---

## Part 2 — How universal principles bite harder under agents

The universal-tier principles still apply. Four of them apply with extra force when the implementer is an agent.

**Small batches matter MORE.** The 2024 DORA report found that AI-assisted coding raises individual output but degrades stability when batch size grows. The discipline of universal Principle 8 is the brake on this. Without it, the productivity gains evaporate. The same DORA work also notes that AI-assisted teams that hold batch size constant see the gains compound rather than offset.

**Boring technology matters MORE.** Agents have seen every framework and every fad in training and reach for the most "interesting" solution unless constrained. Innovation-token budgets shrink: an agent given free choice will burn three tokens where a human would burn one, because every framework looks plausibly applicable. Universal Principle 10 needs harder enforcement in agentic contexts — the default must explicitly be the existing stack, not "what the model thinks is appropriate."

**Review matters MORE.** When an agent generates code at scale, human review is the only place asymmetric judgement is applied. Volume changes the math: a human writing 100 lines an hour is reviewed in proportion to that volume; an agent writing 1000 lines an hour cannot be reviewed by a human at the same proportion. Sub-agent review personas (the third artefact category in this pack) exist because mechanical hooks and embedded skill constraints aren't sufficient — some judgement requires fresh context and an adversarial second pass.

**Operability matters MORE.** An agent thinks it's done at green tests. Universal Principle 1 ("shipped is not done; observed is done") is harder to enforce when the implementer can't observe the production system. The agent needs skills that explicitly include the observation step in their workflow, because there's no on-call instinct to fall back on.

---

## Part 3 — Quick reference

The agentic tier is: an agent's failure modes are predictable, structural, and largely solved by writing them down before the agent encounters them.

The agentic tier is not: a list of complaints about agents, or a denial of how much they accelerate the work.

The eight principles in one line each:

1. Context is the asset; loading it is the work
2. Hallucination is the default; sources are the brake
3. The shortest path is the failure mode; the spec is the seatbelt
4. Evidence beats vibes; "seems right" is never sufficient
5. The rationalisations are predictable; pre-rebut them
6. Stop the line on first failure; never silence the signal
7. Memory lives in artefacts, not in agents
8. Effort is measured in slices and gates, not in calendar time

Cross-references to universal-tier principles that amplify under agents are in Part 2 above.
See `eng-principles-universal.md` for the full tier-1 principles set.
